sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of intermittent flank pain radiating to the groin, which is exacerbated by fluid intake and physical activity.
The pain is described as a 1-4/10, with periods of more intense pain.
The patient reports no fever, nausea, vomiting, or chills.
He has a history of passing kidney stones in the past, including a couple of instances requiring surgery, and has a family history of kidney stones.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of kidney stones, including self-passed stones and surgical interventions.
He has a family history of kidney stones.
Review of systems: The patient reports no symptoms in other organs, including no abdominal tenderness, except for the flank pain and right-sided CVA tenderness.
Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is elevated, likely due to pain, but other vital signs are not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's abdomen is not tender to palpation, except for the right side, where there is CVA tenderness.
Test Results: A CT scan shows a 0.5 cm stone located on the proximal right ureter, with no hydronephrosis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a kidney stone, approximately 0.5 cm in size, located on the proximal right ureter, with intermittent flank pain and CVA tenderness.
Problem list: Kidney stone; active; suspected.
Plan:
I will continue the patient on ibuprofen as needed for pain and prescribe tramadol 50mg as needed for breakthrough pain.
I will also order a BMP, urinalysis, and urine culture to rule out infection.
The patient will continue to use a strainer when urinating to try to catch the stone.
I will see the patient again in 1-2 weeks to reassess symptoms and plan further treatment, including lithotripsy if necessary.
Follow-up: The patient will return in 1-2 weeks for a follow-up appointment.
